questions.text: When a certain radiation passes through matters it lose energy due to:

options.explanation: This option is correct. Radiation can lose energy as it passes through matter due to ionization, which occurs when charged particles (such as alpha or beta particles) collide with atoms in the material, causing them to lose electrons and become ionized.

questions.text: Examples of radiation detector case:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A Geiger counter is a radiation detector that measures ionizing radiation. It detects the presence of radiation by ionizing gas within its chamber when radiation passes through it, producing a measurable electrical pulse.

This is another type of radiation detector used to visualize the passage of ionizing radiation. It operates by creating a supersaturated vapor within a sealed environment. When ionizing particles pass through the chamber, they leave trails of condensation, making their paths visible.

Solid-state detectors use semiconductor materials to detect ionizing radiation. When radiation interacts with the semiconductor material, it generates electron-hole pairs, which create an electric signal that can be measured.

All of the options mentioned (Geiger counter, Wilson cloud chamber, and solid-state detector) are examples of radiation detectors. Each operates on different principles but serves the purpose of detecting and measuring ionizing radiation.

questions.text: Specially designed solid state detector can be used to detect:

This option is correct. A solid-state detector typically involves a reverse-biased p-n junction, where the p-type semiconductor is connected to the negative terminal of a power supply and the n-type semiconductor is connected to the positive terminal.

This option is incorrect. A forward-biased p-n junction occurs when the p-type semiconductor is connected to the positive terminal of a power supply and the n-type semiconductor is connected to the negative terminal. This configuration is not typically used in solid-state detectors.
